text: The surgeon performs an ETEP robotic reef stopa repair on a patient with a recurrent symptomatic hernia. The hernia defect measures approximately seven centimeters wide and ten centimeters long. The surgeon gains direct access to the left retroactive space in the left upper quadrant, using blunt camera dissection to create space for the left mid quadrant port. An ultrasound is used pre-operatively to mark the semilunar lines. A spinal needle assists in finding the trajectory for the port, which is placed lateral to the semilunar line and inserted through the rectus laterally. The camera is positioned in the area for further inferior dissection and space clearing under direct vision with scissors.

The surgeon docks the robot and continues dissecting the medial aspect of the rectus muscle towards the linea alba, preparing to dissect through the posterior rectus sheath. The dissection occurs in the left lower quadrant, with a long trocar placed over the ASIS. The surgeon creates space above the ribs to facilitate the procedure, while managing minor bleeding before cautery is connected.

The surgeon prepares for an upper crossover above the falciform ligament, using a cold cut technique to leave a lip of the posterior rectus towards the linea alba on the ipsilateral side. The dissection progresses inferiorly, revealing the superior aspect of the midline hernia defect, which contains omentum but no bowel. The surgeon opens the left aspect of the posterior rectus sheath attachments to the linea alba, extending below the arcuate line, and continues to cross over the midline inferior to the hernia defect. The hernia sac has been taken down on the ipsilateral side, creating a larger gap in the posterior sheath that will need closure. The surgeon focuses on reducing the incarcerated omentum from the Swiss cheese defects along the left side of the hernia defect.

The surgeon maintains hemostasis as the omentum drops back into the abdominal cavity, ensuring no bleeding is obscured. The right retro-rectus space is developed by entering lateral to the right edge of the hernia defect. The view resembles a trans-abdominal procedure due to the entry into the posterior abdominal cavity after failing to fully reduce the hernia sac.

The surgeon connects the inferior border to the midline below the hernia defect, avoiding the development of the space of Retzius due to the hernia's proximity to the umbilicus. The right retrorectus space is developed out to the semilunar line, revealing a large gap in the posterior sheath that requires closure. A unilateral TAR is performed to accommodate a larger piece of mesh, considering the narrow rectus muscles.

The surgeon identifies a large hernia defect that cannot be closed and prepares to perform a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) on the patient's right side. The space of Bogros is developed to create a tunnel, facilitating the initiation of the TAR procedure. The dissection progresses through the lateral aspect of the posterior sheath, near the semilunar line.

The surgeon approaches the upper abdomen, revealing the transversus abdominis muscle. A combined top-down approach is used to meet the inferior dissection, maintaining meticulous hemostasis. The transversus abdominis is released, with the surgeon making room towards the xiphoid and beneath, completing the division. The dissection continues laterally to allow the posterior sheath to lay flat along the viscera.

The surgeon prepares to close the posterior sheath using a 2-0 V-lock suture, reducing pressure to facilitate the closure. After tying a knot, the surgeon begins closing the anterior defect with an 18-inch V-lock suture, taking several throws to manage suture redundancy. As the closure progresses, the hernia defect is placated.

The surgeon uses their left hand to elevate the left rectus muscle, providing a clear view of the ipsilateral edge of the rectus sheath. The posterior sheath is divided near the linea alba, approximately one centimeter from the defect, to prevent retraction. The surgeon closes a minor diastasis and runs a suture down, joining it with the inferior stitch before tying them together.

The surgeon places a large 30x22 Versatex mesh laparoscopically, extending it further on the side where a unilateral TAR was performed. The mesh covers approximately eight centimeters on the left-hand side, matching the width of the rectus muscle. No drains are used, and the mesh is positioned without additional suture techniques.

The surgeon adjusts the pressure to widen the space, ensuring the mesh sits flat across the dissected retrorectus space. The final inferior corner of the mesh is straightened out, completing the placement.
